In this connection, the trial court ordered that the necessary steps be taken to find out in which prisons M.N.A., S.K. and B.Ç. had been held and ordered an enquiry as to their addresses in the event that they had been released from prison. The trial court further ordered that a confrontation take place once the witnesses had been located. It ordered that photographs be taken of the applicant and A.Y., one from the side and the other from the front, and sent with the reports concerning the case, in the event that M.N.A., S.K. and B.Ç. were located outside its jurisdiction. If they resided in the centre of İzmir, they should be heard in person.

The trial court also ordered that the address of the victim, K.G., be ascertained with a view to bringing him before the court, so that evidence could be taken from him in person.

At a hearing held on 29 June 2004, the trial court heard evidence from S.K. as a witness. S.K. said that he neither knew nor had ever seen the applicant and A.Y. He did not have direct knowledge of K.G.’s shooting and had given his previous statement as a result of information he had obtained from M.N.A. and B.Ç. The trial court reiterated its orders concerning M.N.A., B.Ç. and K.G.

At a hearing held on 26 August 2004, A.Y.’s lawyer submitted a medical report issued by the Elazığ Psychiatric Hospital, dated 3 September 2002, which stated that M.N.A. suffered from depression. The lawyer accordingly asked the trial court not to call M.N.A. as a witness. The trial court did not respond to that request.

Under Law no. 5190 of 16 June 2004, published in the Official Gazette on 30 June 2004, State Security Courts were abolished. The case against the applicant was therefore transferred to the Izmir Assize Court.

On 19 August 2004 M.N.A. was questioned by the Siirt Assize Court, pursuant to a letter rogatory from the trial court. According to the transcript of the hearing at the Siirt Assize Court, M.N.A. was serving a sentence in the Siirt E-type Prison at that time. He explained that in 1992, together with the applicant and a certain A.Y., he had received instructions from the PKK and S.K. to shoot K.G. Accordingly, while he had secured the area, the applicant and A.Y. had gone to shoot K.G. He had heard gunshots but had not seen K.G. being shot. He further identified the applicant and A.Y. from the photographs. The Siirt Assize Court then asked M.N.A. to clarify the inconsistency between the statements he had given to the police and the public prosecutor in 1995 and those he had just given to the court. M.N.A. insisted that he was now telling them the correct version of the events. The court reminded him that in his statement of 29 November 1995 before the public prosecutor he had said that he had not known the accused, and asked him to clarify that point. M.N.A then explained that it was because he had been questioned as an accused on 29 November 1995. He repeated that he was now telling them the correct version of the events.

On 17 December 2004, at the seventh hearing, M.N.A.’s statement was read out. The applicant objected to the statement, referring to the inconsistency of M.N.A.’s statements since 1995. A.Y.’s lawyer also referred to the medical report in respect of M.N.A. and requested that his statement be considered as unreliable. The trial court did not provide a response to that request. It reiterated its orders concerning B.Ç. and K.G.

On 17 February 2005 the eighth hearing was held, and the trial court again reiterated its orders concerning B.Ç. and K.G.

At the ninth hearing on 28 April 2005 the trial court once again reiterated its orders concerning B.Ç. and K.G.

At the tenth hearing on 12 July 2005 the victim, K.G. appeared before the trial court and gave evidence as a witness. K.G. submitted that he had not seen the applicant or A.Y. at the crime scene and that they had not been involved in his shooting. He gave a detailed description of the people who had shot him.

On 4 October 2005 the Izmir Assize Court found the applicant guilty as charged and convicted him on the basis of the case file as a whole, including the record of the reconstruction of the events and the report of the photo identification.

The court accordingly sentenced the applicant to life imprisonment, under Article 125 of the former Criminal Code, for carrying out activities with the aim of bringing about the secession of part of the national territory.

On 14 February 2006 the Court of Cassation quashed the judgment and remitted the case to the first-instance court in order to determine whether the terms of the new Criminal Code, which had entered into force on 1 June 2005 (no. 5237), were more favourable for the applicant. The case was thus re-examined by the Izmir Assize Court in view of the recent legislative changes.

On 13 June 2006 the applicant’s lawyer requested that the trial court bring M.N.A before the court in order to cross-examine him directly and arrange a physical confrontation between him and the applicant. The Izmir Assize Court rejected the request on the grounds that it had already examined and rejected the same request.

On the same date the Izmir Assize Court, relying on the same pieces of evidence, once again found the applicant guilty as charged under Article 125 of the Criminal Code and sentenced him to life imprisonment.

The applicant lodged an appeal on 3 July 2003, in which his lawyer submitted, inter alia, that M.N.A.’s statements should not have been taken into consideration given the medical report on his mental state. He further submitted that the applicant had been deceived by the police and asked the Court of Cassation to declare his statements inadmissible and in contravention of Article 6 of the Convention.

On 19 December 2006 the Court of Cassation rejected the applicant’s appeal.

1.0: The implications of the ECHR's judgments on cases of wrongful convictions in the United Kingdom have far-reaching consequences, not only for those directly affected but also for the legal framework governing criminal justice in England and Wales. A systematic review of the casework of Lord Reed revealed significant deficiencies in the application of the standard of proof in such cases, allowing the wrongful convictions to be upheld by lower courts, even in cases where the original trial had previously been overturned on appeal. Such findings underscore the need for a more effective framework to ensure that the standard of proof in criminal cases becomes better aligned with the evidentiary standards outlined in the ECHR.

"During the latest reading of Mr. Smith's claim against General Secretary of State for the Home Department, the judge asked the witness whose statement had triggered the proceedings to elaborate on his account of the events. In response, the witness detailed the circumstances leading up to the night when his property was demolished by local authorities, highlighting discrepancies with the official explanation provided by the authorities. The solicitor, who observed the events from a safe distance, noted the defendant's representatives failed to take steps to prevent destruction of evidence and questioned the necessity of the demolition. The judge probed further into the case after the plaintiff asserted he had been relocated to a temporary accommodation without sufficient means of sustaining himself and providing adequate housing options. In light of these discrepancies, the judge scheduled an inquiry into the decision to demolish the property, thereby upholding the citizen's claim that the authorities' actions fell short of meeting minimum standards of procedural justice and public order procedures."

The court of great nations once again held itself up to scrutiny in the case of the Eriole Shipping Company Ltd. v. The Republic of Turkey, a suit brought under Article 4 of the ECHR claiming protection from unfair interference with Mr. Oguz's right to a private life, as defined in Article 8 of the Convention. The claimant sought reparations for substantial financial losses incurred after Turkey commenced a lengthy investigation into Eriole, accusing the company and everyone involved of illegal activities; further complications arose during the procedure, further threatening Mr. Oguz's economic well-being in a manner deemed to be disproportionate to the legitimate objectives pursued by the state in relation to those found guilty, yet the state's claims may also arguably have been unjust in pursuing a remedy.

The dismissal of an eviction notice was upheld by the Court after a successful judicial review of the decision, with the hearing on judicial discretion resulting in the plaintiff having to endure rent deposits left unpaid for nine months, a five-month eviction that never came into force after the relevant term.

Although procedural grievances , though differing depending on the context often trigger the jurisdiction of the ECHR , where no dispute exists , human rights may be violated , most cases are in fact driven by claims made by individuals against the State under Articles 17 and 21 , or by various non state parties and private individuals under various articles including , though this is of less common occurrence . It is not until in cases of violations by the majority being in the minority or for the Court to determine that it needs to order compliance action by the state .

In a landmark case that has sent shockwaves throughout the European legal community, the European Court of Human Rights has ruled in favour of a group of disabled individuals who were denied inclusion in a major parliamentary training course due to inadequate accessibility measures. While the court found that the UK government had taken steps to address the issue, it was deemed that the accommodations made were insufficient and resulted in the applicants taking extensive periods away from work without pay, thereby severely impacting their livelihoods. The ruling highlights the importance of ensuring equal access to education and employment opportunities and underscores the need for consistent and proactive measures to be implemented at all stages of the development of legislation, education programs and training initiatives.

In a landmark decision, the European Court of Human Rights emphasised that all individuals have an "essential interest in the preservation and transmission of cultural heritage" and that this interest outweighed the need for compensation in cases where natural heritage sites with negative historical and cultural connections were demolished. Notably, the court did not rule that historic sites could never be demolished, highlighting the complex and nuanced nature of the situation.

In a case that highlights the tensions between individual rights and state security, the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) is set to review the decision of the Court of Appeal in England and Wales regarding a man who, under conditions of heightened threat, was deemed unable to exercise his right to free assembly under the Article 11 of the ECHR. The case originated before the High Court, where the defendant was denied bail due to a previous conviction under the Anti-Terrorism Act 2000, allowing the authorities to restrict his freedom at the request of the Investigatory Powers and Security Act. With his application for leave to appeal turned down, the defendant challenged this decision as it would violate the Convention's free assembly provisions and Article 19 by potentially limiting public gatherings beyond a narrow exemption. Though considering the extreme threat posed by terrorist activities while safeguarding national security, the ECHR Grand Chamber reviews the lower court's analysis for adherence to the ECHR, weighing public freedoms against safeguards of national security and safety under Articles 8 and 10.

The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly underscored that access to information under Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights is not absolute, but also that restrictions must meet stringent tests of necessity and proportionality. In this context, the restrictions should be confined solely to cases where there is a clear and present danger, and should be proportionate to this danger. Such restrictions can serve no other purpose than to achieve the legitimate aim of averting harm, should be no more extensive than necessary, and should not arbitrarily infringe upon the fundamental right of freedom of expression.

In accordance with the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, a man's right to freedom of expression outweighs the need for national security, as exemplified by the cases on these pages. The Austrian Federal Constitutional Court's application of the notion of necessity in defending freedom of speech highlights the balance the judiciary must strike to ensure the protection of human rights, while upholding the rule of law. Furthermore, the Committee for Legal and Social Policy of Poland's ruling exemplifies the ECHR's role in upholding the rights of vulnerable populations, with a focus on the preservation of equal opportunity for all citizens.

In considering the landmark case of Loizou v. United Kingdom, delivered in 2004, it brings to forefront questions surrounding the establishment of a two-tier structure under Article 10 of ECHR - one governing freedom of expression itself in the press and the other concerning hate speech on television. The rationale for choosing this case emerges from the earlier ruling in United Broadcasting of Ireland v Smith, wherein the High Court of Ireland held that an individual could bring a claim to court under Article 10 if she had been held in contempt by a press organization due to her defamatory writings, prompting further scrutiny and resulting in case before the ECHR.

Judge Corte delitosas considered it permissible for the Dutch authorities to have intercepted e-mails of individuals , without a warrant citing the urgency of being bound by international treaty obligations and national standards , being part of a criminal case at the time . Given the European Court of Human Rights' jurisdiction, in the case of Smith et al. v. United Kingdom, the concept of foreseeability played a pivotal role in validating the state's measures as a means of protecting the right to an effective means of redress for victims of human rights violations.The European Court's interpretation in this precedent has carried significant weight, particularly with regards to Article 2 of the Convention regarding the right to life, where the authorities must exercise the power to arrest, detain, and execute life-imprisonment judgments based on probabilities of guilt and necessity rather than suspicion to align with the principles of humane treatment.A range of jurisdictions have sought to draw inspiration from the court's standard, refining their sentencing procedures to balance public security with the fundamental human right to freedom from inexcusable liberty deprivation. Although the court's stance is often cautiously nuanced, it serves as a bulwark for upholding justice by safeguarding those rights, even when the threshold of "more favourable consequences than any other choice had offered" may be difficult to sustain in specific circumstances under varying factual contexts.

The Court's order dated 13 October 2022, while quashing the appeal, quashed the order the respondent international trade union had obtained from the High Court of Georgia. The case regarded the claimant’s plea against the respondent state following his deportation from Russia to Azerbaijan via the border of Georgia where he had stayed under international protection of the French diplomatic missions and security of an ECHR representative accompanying and representing him.

"The European Court of Human Rights' landmark case of Valker v. the United Kingdom reaffirmed the crucial distinction between 'not having a reasonable alternative option' and 'being in the situation of being denied reasonable access', indicating a need for caution in exercising rights under article 6(1) ECHR without disregarding the necessity to ensure reasonable access to justice in such cases, the judgment provided further insight into the importance of weighing the competing interests of the right to a fair trial against the limitations of providing reasonable alternatives, in an effort to alleviate the burden on the judicial system and to bring justice to those whose rights were abridged due to circumstance, without allowing such nuances to obscure the fundamental principles guiding the application of the ECHR."

The recent controversy surrounding the European Court of Human Rights' rejection of an appeal from an elderly man who faced deportation to Romania has raised questions about the compatibility of Article 13 of the Charter with the case law established by the ECHR in the landmark judgement in N v Ireland in 1996; it is submitted that the applicant having been wrongly identified is an imminent threat to public safety, therefore Article 39 of the Charter must be relied upon. The facts of this present case appear to mirror those of N and do therefore fall within the ambit of the principle established in this case. Furthermore, it can be reasonably argued as per the judgment in Z and that since the applicant was facing deportation to a country in which they spoke little they could face particular hardship or suffering. Consequently, Article 7 must be applied, thus demonstrating the necessity of balancing rights – a balancing power which must be exercised in accordance with the overall public interest.

After analyzing various decisions from the European Court of Human Rights, it has become increasingly clear that the lack of transparency within government agencies can often lead to significant delays in the processing and implementation of policy changes. In the 2014 Hirst v United Kingdom case, this issue was highlighted, with the court ruling that unreasonable waiting times for prison bail hearings infringed on Article 6(1) of the European Convention. As a result, the UK government was forced to reform its bail procedures, introducing procedures that prioritize efficiency and fairness, thus upholding the rule of law. This marked a significant shift towards greater transparency and accountability within these institutions.

The ECHR plays a pivotal role in upholding the human rights of individuals across the continent by considering applications of individuals claiming non-compliance with the European Convention on Human Rights through its court proceedings. The court serves as a crucial check on state power, safeguarding fundamental freedoms and liberties in myriad interconnected domestic regimes. With an archive of thousands of cases submitted annually, each decision embodies the court's rigorous commitment to determining the proportionality of a state's legislation in relation to the Convention's tenets, effectively shaping the legal landscape across jurisdictions.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled in favor of an individual from Lithuania, finding that a public health regulation had breached their right to respect for private and family life under Article 8 of the European Convention. In 2001, human rights activists from Ukraine, seeking greater autonomy and closer alignment with Western standards, applied to the ECHR for protection against restrictions on participation in cultural education imposed by the Ukrainian authorities. However, the applicants alleged that the authorities consistently interfered with their right to freedom of expression through the persecution of several pro-EU and pro-European identity activists. Specifically, the security services had detained individuals attending cultural and political events, searched their homes, monitored their online communications and denied them access to websites and news outlets critical of the Ukrainian government. Drawing on Article 10 of the ECHR, the applicants argued that the search, detention and monitoring were not carried out in accordance with the law, violated their rights to freedom of expression and freedom of assembly and association. The Court's broad reading of the article drew on its previous case law, including Budge v the United Kingdom (2021), which found that while the interference must be "aimed directly and absolutely at the exercise or abrogation of said rights ..., it also must be compatible with it."

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently held that the disclosure of state-funded journalistic sources under the Freedom of Information Acts is crucial for journalists exercising their right to report the news freely. In the case of Handyside and Others v United Kingdom (1987), the court ruled that the protection afforded by Article 8 extended beyond individuals against arbitrary detention, stating that it also encompasses the protection of sources who may be vulnerable to ill-treatment at the hands of law enforcement agencies; therefore governments must ensure that they implement appropriate safeguards to balance the public's right to information against individual privacy and sources at risk of harm in disclosing the identities of journalistic sources where the source is identifiable from official documents.

**Case T 142/19: Nikolai Nikolaevich versus Russia** In a highly controversial ruling, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) delivered a landmark judgment in the case of **Nikolai Nikolaevich versus Russia**, highlighting the institution's steadfast commitment to upholding human rights and ensuring that individuals are not subjected to incommunicado detention for extended periods in the absence of effective means of implementation. The court ruled that Article 5(3)(c) of the European Convention on Human Rights necessitates the ECHR to assess whether the grounds for incommunicado detention are still valid within the limited timeframe of two months as contemplated by the Convention, in this instance. Due to the uncooperative nature of the state's actions, in the opinion of the court, the applicant was subjected to arbitrary detention despite an initial 70-day detention in incommunicado conditions prior to their surrender in Poland under formal arrangements in response to a European Information Centre summons. In a rare instance of divergent legal interpretation within the ECHR, the applicant now seeks that the court declare their detention unlawful because they have in fact already taken steps towards reintroduction to society in the form of regular visits to family but not being allowed out of the country. During a landmark hearing before the European Court of Human Rights, the applicant, a human rights barrister, argued that the detention measure had breached his Charter of Fundamental Rights as the restrictive conditions made it impossible for him to receive essential medical treatment. The first time he required such care, his detainers had deliberately withheld his access, claiming that a transfer to the hospital was against his disciplinary policy. According to reports, he was placed under solitary confinement and subjected to hard labour in excess of the prescribed one hour for every non-transient illness or injury, as the medical staff refused to see him due to fear of retaliation from his cellmates. Upon appealing to his national court and being refused the right there, both appeals decisions were dismissed along with an enforcement order and the barrister was subjected to torture for seven more months, causing irreversible damage to his physical health. In pleading his case to the ECHR, he claimed that his rights under article 3 of the European Convention were flouted and his detention was illegal, and that the prison authorities were in violation of Article 13 and Article 2.

The courts of the European Union have consistently upheld the principle of transparency in the transfer of individuals from one state to another in cases where the person concerned carries out an activity which could be a breach of the other state's sovereignty and where the transfer is considered to be in the public interest according to the guidelines emanating from the Court of Justice of the European Union, such as cases involving transfers for public policy or to protect public security or to prevent infringement of public policy of a non-discriminatory nature or in cases dealing with public security related concerns for counterterrorism such as in counterterrorist financing, all in accordance with the principles of proportionality and necessity in these circumstances typically where the individual has not raised irreversible harm and are not confined to only serious crime cases, although examples like extradition to United Kingdom on sexual assault for serious cases where the person would face lengthy sentences for minor crimes in another jurisdiction or is in the context of a diplomatic asylum claim where the individual may receive enhanced security guarantees in another jurisdiction.

In the landmark case of Smith v. The United Kingdom, the ECHR held that a failure by a state to ensure the effective enforcement of a right protected under Article 8 of the Convention did not amount to a breach of that right if the state had taken all necessary steps to do so in circumstances exceeding the control of the individual and beyond its capacities. This reasoning in the judgment can be seen as an extension of the principle seen in the precedent case of Chikafunda v. The United Kingdom, which established that the responsibility of a state to ensure that effective enforcement actions were taken to reclaim its own property after a decision of its courts was likewise conditional upon the existence of circumstances necessitating such actions. While such reasoning was disputed as too narrow by several national courts, it continues to have implications for the interpretation of Article 6 and has been cited in numerous subsequent decisions under these two Articles.

UK, the European Court of Human Rights adjudicated that the denial of access to judicial process was not only a subjective experience but a fundamental right, as stipulated in Article 6 of the ECHR. Bulgaria, the European Court of Human Rights underscored the importance of respecting an individual's right to education by holding that the country's education system's opaque and underfunded status affected the quality of educational services available to students with disabilities. In two separate cases, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has concluded that the state's responsibility for the acts of its armed forces only extends to the borders of its territory; if foreign military forces enter a country outside those borders without an explicit invitation or tacit consent, the ECHR ruled that the state is not responsible for their actions, as per Article 5, Section 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights. In a case in which foreign military personnel had killed a Russian citizen in Germany based on a false tip, the ECHR ultimately held that the German state was liable, ruling that "as a matter of principle its jurisdiction over its territory can be invoked by acts committed on foreign soil with the help of its agents as well -- for as long as such acts are related to or are emanating from its territory." This concept stands in contrast with traditional notions of jurisdiction , since it does not rely on the location of a host country's armed forces; in countries that traditionally maintained their territorial control over foreign military bases, as long as the military personnel's activities did not involve an extensive deployment on permanent basis, in territory over which state had any jurisdiction.

A landmark decision in the history of human rights, the Björnsgaard decision marked a turning point in the European Court of Human Rights' approach to balancing traditional notions of state sovereignty with European international law. This precedent set a unique precedent, highlighting the importance of incorporating contextual understanding in the interpretation and implementation of treaty provisions, especially in cases where human rights protections sometimes conflict with domestic legal frameworks. The ruling underscored the pressing need for judicial bodies to develop a nuanced approach, one that is grounded in a deep understanding of the complexities involved, and acknowledges the far-reaching implications of their decisions on the lives of individuals affected by state action.

In assessing the European Court of Human Rights' jurisprudence on the protection of linguistic rights, it is paramount to examine the seminal case of Chukwumelundu v. Nigeria [1995] 23 Eur. Ct. HR. Rep. 121. While the case primarily discussed the interpretation of Article 13 of the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, the ruling established the legal precedent for adjudicating claims of linguistic discrimination in national context. This authority is often invoked to inform the evaluation of requests for international protection grounded in Article 10, protecting freedom of expression, in situations where linguistic discrimination may ensue. Subsequent judicial decisions in the ECHR would build upon this fundamental principle, iteratively specifying the circumstances under which an individual's ability to communicate peacefully with their government is deemed essential for the protection of human rights.

Although the notion of ' privacy ' in European law has had a complex history , it evolved significantly following the publication of the Convention in 1950 . In such cases , different courts and governments have divergent legal interpretations of " privacy " , a fact which makes interpretation difficult .

In a landmark decision, the ECHR reaffirmed the principle of right to a fair trial, ruling against Poland over its strict laws governing online privacy. In a landmark decision delivered yesterday, the European Court of Human Rights underscored the pivotal role of individual subjective experience in shaping the interpretation of Article 1 of Protocol No. 4 of the European Convention for Human Rights, as applied to the dismissal of a detainee from the detention center following his failed asylum application. Prior to the hearing, the state authorities had requested that the Court consider an additional element not already before the court, notwithstanding the fact that such did not conform to the necessary requirements of Article 36(1) or the principles of res judicata. Pursuant to these circumstances, the Commission of the European Court of Human Rights accepted the case and subsequently submitted the matter to the Court, clarifying the Court's jurisdiction in light of established jurisprudence on the notion of provisional measures under Article 39(3) and the principles of national law. The European Court of Human Rights recently refused to hear an appeal in a case alleging discriminatory treatment by a British employer towards a pregnant female plaintiff, citing her pregnancy as an exception to a company-wide policy of only hiring male firefighters. The case, similar to the previous Pinto Industrial Plastics Ltd [2010] ECR-500, highlighted the need for equal consideration of gender when employment regulations are applied, especially during pregnancy. In terms of the rules of engagement, the relevant case law such as Pears v UK [2001] ECR-3631, shows that the European Court supports pregnant women in similar positions by granting exception under same sex discrimination rules to same or analogous women applicants facing denial of requested protections.

An English language court ruling issued by the European Court of Human Rights noted a precedent-breaking case in which a convicted individual was acquitted following claims of unlawful detention under domestic law in the Irish state as the Court deemed the act to be incompatible with Article 5 which defines the 'right to liberty and security', and by Article 3 regarding 'protection of the body and dignity', and applied the principles set forth by Article 47 of the European Convention on Human Rights, relating to right to effective legal remedy for unlawful acts, all according to the 1998 European Court ruling .

The European Court of Human Rights has recently received a case that revolves around the interpretation of Article 7 of the European Convention, which guarantees the right to freedom of assembly. In this case, a group of protesters are seeking damages for being detained by the police for several hours while demonstrating peacefully. The applicants claim that the detention was unlawful as it took place in an area where protests are generally not tolerated and that the police failed to provide adequate reasons for the prolonged detention. The court will now scrutinize the evidence to determine whether the applicant's rights to freedom of assembly were ultimately violated.

The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) yesterday ruled in favour of a Russian migrant who had been denied entry to the UK due to concerns he may pose a threat to national security and crime prevention. The court held that Article 15 of the 1948 Convention is not limited to states' freedom from external influence and that UK immigration authorities may indeed consider foreign-born individuals more vulnerable due to limited social integration with the local population and lack of cultural background in the country. This was in response to a complaint by one Ilyas Ziyadinovich, a 30-year-old from Uzbekistan, who was turned back at the border nine months ago. Mr Ziyadinovich held a master's degree in social work but could not pass the language test to prove his integration into British society. After entering Romania, Ziyadinovich reported arriving in Ukraine and joined the conflict because he sought a safe place to study and live peacefully.

The application of Article 17 by the UK Supreme Court in the case of D.P. v. Council of the Association of British Railways Transport Executive Schools (2013) provides important guidance. This decision highlights the importance of individuals with an illness or disability who possess a part-time bus driving licence being offered equivalent provision that can help them access post-16 education and being free to join the travel by offering a combined annual licence offering a day ticket that complies with rail vouchers being available under a non discriminatory conditions. This guidance shows that the Supreme Court can offer an authoritative insight into the interaction between EU directives and the Common Travel Area.

The European Court of Human Rights has finally rendered its judgment in the landmark case ECHR/2018/1034/UK, citing Article 2 (Right to Life) and Article 3 (Prohibition from torture) of the Convention. In this verdict, the Court set a compelling precedent by ruling that two British security measures – counter-terrorism detention without trial and preventive detention without charge – violated the Human Rights.

Case C-151/15 De Gea, et au Roi on the application for injunctive interim measures, decided on 11 December 2015. As the applicant has been serving voluntary military service in a United Nations peace-keeping mission for the last 14 months, the Court has available all information to the Registrar. The application in main proceedings by the applicant for interim relief, sought on grounds of exhaustion of other remedies invoked last in connection with a decision of the Court that, on 9 March 2014, had dismissed her application for a prohibition in the context of a dispute as to the right of the applicant to the dignity of human life under the Contract as interpreted by the European Court of Human Rights in the case Hirst v United Kingdom (Application 75532/08) and relating events that the applicants had endured in arbitrary detention subsequent to her release from prison, referred back for further examination. It was claimed that the applicant had been subject to inhuman conditions and forced to endure incommunicado detention.

An evaluation of the recent ruling in case of A. v. United Kingdom by the European Court of Human Rights serves as a model for understanding the impact on various aspects of national law, with implications for the protection of the right to a private life. The court noted that the admissibility of certain evidence in these cases depends heavily on whether it falls under an exception to Article 8 - a fundamental principle that safeguards an individual's right to a private existence. Many of these judgments highlight the complexity and challenges posed by balancing individual rights with the responsibilities and demands of public safety. In doing so, the ECHR case law provides crucial guidance for the application of Article 8 in everyday life and in judicial proceedings itself.

"In the absence of explicit exemptions, section 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights requires states to investigate and prosecute any government officials found guilty of violating the right to life, with the result being significant legal precedents for justice and accountability, despite ongoing calls for abolition of the immunity enjoyed by heads of state and government ministers."
Having worked in the English judicial system for some 45 years, there were few things Pinochet could not do.

The Advisory Committee of Human Rights stated unequivocally that the country was in breach of its obligations under the European convention by granting permission and subsequent instructions under Article 17 were ineffective and could not outweigh the absolute right to life conferred in Article 2.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld a landmark precedent, ruling that the use of reasonable grounds for expulsion is necessary to ensure the orderly functioning of higher education institutions in times of crisis.

In a landmark decision issued on January 19, 2005, a panel of three judges at the first instance reconsidered the ground of non-discrimination in discrimination arising from an unjust enrichment when the employee , with five years' seniority and after being passed over , had left to pursue a career as a human rights officer at an international organisation in Geneva . The ECHR had already determined that the Appellant's rights had been violated by the respondent employer , and the claimant had sought damages , but on the question of a financial award , the lower court had stated that he relied on " special considerations of conduct " on the part of an alleged victim . The ECHR found in answer to the claim " whether these considerations are sufficient for an individual case , or why they , in fact , do not concern the alleged victim and for this purpose , they are entirely unrelated " . The ECHR found the claimant unable to demonstrate any form of connection which would entail the conduct considered the alleged victim , thereby dismissing the further application . As they interpreted these findings the ECHR observed that the lower court followed an analysis from a hypothetical question for the protection of personal data as the basis to assess whether access to private data may trigger the protection right under the Data Protection Act , thus contrary to

Given the European Court of Human Rights' recent decisions, it has been made clear that the rights of individuals, particularly those from minority groups, are not being adequately protected within the United Kingdom's domestic framework. Article 14 of the European Convention requires that states take measures to prevent discrimination based on certain grounds, yet the UK's law often falls short in this regard. The most recent case, involving a migrant from Eastern Europe, emphasizes the urgent need for the UK to address this issue. In the eyes of the ECHR judges, this case highlights the crucial role of effective remedies in preventing disparate treatment. They underscore that states have an obligation to investigate and respond to allegations of discrimination promptly. The courts also stress the importance of protecting individuals' rights, especially those of minority groups, from violating or abetting discriminating ideologies in the UK society. This highlights the intricate complexities of applying the European Convention to the UK legislation and need for stricter protections to be in place for minority groups that has become rapidly apparent in recent case law. Lastly, it sets the stage for any potential appeal of the case, emphasizing the need for the UK to revise its disability laws and provide a more inclusive environment for the individuals involved.

The European Court of Human Rights recently delivered a landmark judgment in the case of Jansa Oates v Kingdom of Spain, ruling that the Spanish government's measure restricting access to online information was a breach of Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court found that the restrictions, imposed under the general surveillance law, were excessively broad and disproportionately limited the ability of individuals to exercise freedom of expression, particularly for those who relied on the internet for information and opinions.

The European Court of Human Rights ruled unanimously that the blanket exclusion of asylum claimants from jobs within six months of the decision led to arbitrary and unjustified discrimination, contrary to Article 14 of the Convention. The applicant was a Syrian national who applied as an asylum seeker in June 2015 but failed the initial application due to difficulties in proof of ongoing conflict and no connections with the applicants family. After the applicant attended a language training session to acquire English and participate in an interview with his former teacher to secure a reference. There was an objection on the grounds of a background check that was necessary due to the application and job was initially not granted but he had the opportunity to appeal.

The European Court of Human Rights has long been instrumental in championing the rights of litigants across the globe, tirelessly working to uphold the European Convention on Human Rights. A recent case brought before the court by a Danish citizen, Mr. Nielsen, raises critical questions about the right to freedom of expression. In 2018, Mr. Nielsen, a vocal anti-immigration activist, was granted access to a public rooftop, where he prepared a press conference to protest rising migrant numbers. Despite this, local authorities forcefully prevented the gathering, citing public order concerns. Mr. Nielsen subsequently brought a claim to the ECHR, arguing that the eviction infringed his freedom of expression. The court ruled that Article 11 of the convention was breached in the application of the 11th chapter of the First Additional Protocol, recognizing that public gatherings of a peaceful and spontaneous nature were essential to a democratic society. Furthermore, in consideration of this judgment, Article 10 of the convention shall also be taken into account and that it must be balanced against the aim of maintaining public order. This precedent serves as a clear indication that public authorities are under obligation to protect the right to freedom of expression but with the understanding that this must not breach or unduly constrain public security, order, or morals at

The European Court of Human Rights on Monday ruled that the United Kingdom must ensure that asylum seekers are not detained indefinitely pending deportation by maintaining a system of bail or bail-like alternatives, but still facing stricter conditions for access to accommodation and healthcare.

Counsel for the applicant challenged a decision by the Republic of Poland denying him claim contained in a legal action for remedy before the Office of complainant at the European Court of Human Rights. The applicant, Mr Ogiełowicz, submits that the denial of his claim in the matter between Poland and Mr Ogiełowicz , involving potential deprivation of liberty at a hearing in which he did not have the assistance of an interpreter , was in violation of his right to a fair trial and his right to effective remedy under Article 6 § 3 ( a ) andArticle 13 of the European Convention relating to effective remedy , as interpreted by a number of judgments .

On 28 February 2001, the ECHR held that "Lemon Law" in Article 5 of the Federal Republic of Germany's Basic Law which in its entirety has been upheld on account of Article 17 in accordance with Germany and the Federal Republic of Germany made changes to the law bringing it's provisions in article 4 in line with the Convention . However the ECHR did not prevent Germany from changing it .

In order to determine whether the right to education under Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights can be exercised by individuals in detention, the court must consider the specific circumstances of each case and weigh the balance between the right to education and the need for security to prevent harm to the detainee or the general public. The European Court of Human Rights has recognized that detention can limit an individual's ability to pursue education, particularly in cases where detention is prolonged and the detention center lacks appropriate educational facilities. In some cases, such as the isolated conditions in Alleston v United Kingdom, the court has relied on the derogation from Article 2(1) of Protocol 4, which allows measures derogating from Article 2 under exceptional circumstances. However, in determining the duration of detention, the court considers whether measures have been taken to ensure the detained individual's access to medical and educational resources.

"The European Court of Human Rights' landmark decision in the Smith case shed light on the precarious fine line between state interference and artistic expression, famously invoking the famous quote ' artistic licence ' in a fervent appeal for increased press freedom following France's 2007 Act Against Hate Online Speech legislation. However, it was only eight years later that the Grand Chamber's decision set the definitive stage for this contentious territory in the case of @ @ Office for Democratic Institutions and human rights v. Russia, ultimately invalidating the controversial Article 15 restrictions and providing a clear directive that freedom of assembly and expression should preclude even the government's power to ban or confiscate its possession of its literature under specific but justified circumstances. "Unlike other international human rights bodies, the European Court of Human Rights functions as a judicial body with an equal degree of independence and functional autonomy from its state of reference," stated Chief Justice Jan Malenovski. "The independence of the judiciary is thus a powerful incentive for its court to be free of political considerations in the exercise of its power of judicial review," thereby safeguarding the most indispensable basic rights for the protection of human dignity.

The European Court of Human Rights emphasised that a person's conviction is solely based on the proceedings of an impartial tribunal to ensure justice. Though, in the case of Louth v United Kingdom (2004), the Court noted that an "innocent man" convicted by a UK court might be subject to a life imprisonment sentence. The ECHR highlighted in case reports that the Court examined five main aspects of criminal cases and conviction (the case of Godefroy v United Kingdom 2008).

In reaching a judgment in case no. 44547/12 concerned against Mr Demirkos, the European Court of Human Rights, sitting as a Grand Chamber in a judgment of 31 January 2013, held that the prohibition of torturing or using degrading treatment under Article 3 not only concerns the physical use of force to cause physical suffering or death, but also concerns any treatment directed towards an individual on the grounds deemed unlawful, unjust and inhuman, such as forced deportations and sexual assaults made to another person, such as the applicant’s spouse.

The European Court of Human Rights was established on 5 May 1949 to ensure protection of the fundamental human rights of individuals within the member states of the Council of Europe under the convention, the European convention adopted in 1950, and to safeguard the independence and impartiality of courts of "final instance competent in the member states of that convention," the member states agreed to establish a permanent institution for the protection of human rights (European Court of Human Rights) that would hear appeals on the basis of the convention adopted two years earlier (1953), as well as other similar conventions that most of the member states had signed or acceded to prior to their membership.

The European Court of Human Rights has been the decisive forum for the examination of nearly all human rights disputes for over six decades, resulting in legal precedents and interpretations that have significantly influenced both international and European Union law. One of the key developments in the human rights agenda in the Court was the establishment of the Group of Friends of the Chairpersons of the Court, which has assisted the Court in communicating its judgments to national parliaments and governments and the respective national human rights institutions. More recently, several European governments and human rights institutions took the lead in taking steps to strengthen the role of civil society in human rights matters ahead of the European Parliament's role in the human rights agenda and in decision-making by the European Court. However, according to several court precedents, the European Convention on human rights is binding only upon the governments of those states that have ratified it and has not yet had full legislative and judicial effect in some of these same states.

"In today's hearing, the court deliberated over the case of Khuzhirin versus Russia, grappling with the fine line between national security and individual privacy. The challenge arose from the Russian government's appropriation of regional communication records without explicit judicial oversight, sparking controversy over the balance between state power and civil liberties. In a ruling that has drawn widespread attention, the ECHR has emphasized the importance of adhering to the principles enshrined in Article 10 of the European Convention, guaranteeing freedom of expression, particularly in times of social turmoil. The court ultimately held that the Russian authorities did not sufficiently justify the imposition of restrictions on the right to freedom of expression, finding the measure to have been disproportionate to the danger posed, thus prompting the state to revise its policies to reiterate the significance of such protections.

In his written declaration to the European Court of Human Rights, the former Ukrainian president Viktor Yanukovych claimed that he had no knowledge of any illegal activities and was under a lot of pressure from law enforcement agencies, suggesting that he cooperated fully with the investigation and committed 400,000 hours in the interrogation after the results were publicly published, that the last document that was sent over his signature was 'the letter of resignation'.

The European Court of Human Rights has established that the application of the European Convention on Human Rights to a given case is dependent on the domestic legal framework in which it operates, and therefore the outcome of a case under the Convention cannot be predicated solely on the application of general human rights principles; in order for an applicant who is affected by one of protected rights under the Convention to be entitled to seek redress under the Court, all these circumstances must be considered, with consideration given to the provisions of the particular domestic legislation or case law under which the domestic state government holds that they are in compliance in order to warrant it. In doing so, we must rely on the extensive case law of the ECHR which contains numerous precedents on issues such as different measures needed for effective social policy objectives with relevantly applicable legislation . In instances where the application of ECHR precluded the full implementation of national measures due to the non-compliance with rights under the European Convention in specific cases , prior ECHR rulings have established the importance of reconciliation through legislative amendment or implementation where legally binding interpretations do not render the ECHR otherwise effective for legislative requirements . In the judgment delivered on the 10th of February, 2019, Commission Ms. Christine Müller, head of the ECHR, stated, 'There is no reasonable suspicion' of misconduct towards Mr. A in the course of the investigation conducted by prosecutors concerning the events of the 20th of October, 2005, where they fell into a coma due to excessive force after the arrest of a male companion. She highlighted, 'Some parts of the testimony had presented elements that appeared similar to confessions', which the prosecution had apparently tried to argue. Ms Smith, acting as counsel for an unidentified complainant, stated in the court report, 'The whole of the subsequent psychiatric treatment was highly ineffective,' because at some time that followed he came out of the coma in time for the trial to begin.

"A critical examination of the ECHR case of S. versus the United Kingdom, released in 2015, revealed a striking trend of dismissal of allegations of systemic violence against women in British armed forces units. The court's ruling noted that while the issue remained "unstated and unwritten" in military recruitment and selection processes, "it would be 'a gross dereliction of duty' to fail to address this. Authorities must prioritize gender equality and prevention of violence in the armed forces to live up to the standards enshrined in ECHR articles 3, 11 and 14 to counter the prevalent pattern of abuse exhibited by the military."

The European Court of Human Rights, in a landmark decision, held that states are under an obligation to ensure that visitors to its premises, including the ECHR's own chambers, receive access to information on how to appeal their cases. Article 34 of the ECHR states that in such cases, individuals have the ability to appeal to the ECHR. However, the scope of this right has been a contentious issue, particularly in countries with limited media coverage of court proceedings and a lack of transparency. Several applicants to the ECHR attempted to seek the disclosure of the decisions and have been met with resistance from European governments. The Court has rejected these claims while also issuing a directive to the national authorities to ensure that the rights are upheld in accordance with the case law principles. The right to access the proceedings is viewed as fundamental to holding government accountable for infringement of individual rights. This decision represents a significant increase in the frequency of these kinds of cases, emphasizing the need for effective mechanisms and support structures to provide better access to justice.

According to the European Court of Human Rights, the principle of non-discrimination must be interwoven throughout all aspects of a country's national laws and policies to ensure that individuals are treated equally, regardless of their nationality, race, gender, age, or ethnicity.

"In an unprecedented move, the grand chamber of the ECHR has made a bold decision to nullify the deportation of a citizen of Romania, sparking widespread debate among human rights advocates who have termed it a 'landmark victory for the rule of law.' According to the court's rationale, the member state failed to adhere to its obligations under part two of the convention, culminating in the dismissal of the original appeal. However, the decision raises questions regarding the application of article eight as it remains uncertain whether the applicant's removal from the country constitutes a breach of their right to be free from expulsion except when justified by just causes.

The European Court of Human Rights has played a pivotal role in shaping the international human rights landscape by providing a platform for individuals to seek redress for alleged violations of their civil and political rights. Established in 1957, the Court is the primary judicial body responsible for the implementation of the European Convention on Human rights on individuals residing in European countries who are bound by its provisions <https://www.curia.europa.eu/en/aboutthem/section/section/bilateral-review/2018/may/text/publication/The-european-court-of-human-rights>. With 50 members states as currently designated members, each member state is required to submit an international treaty on their own behalf, while also ensuring individual complaints to the court for review of human rights violations against any state are not at first refusal [1].

An applicant's request for extension of time until seven months after filing a complaint had recently been declined and the circumstances of the case were particularly complicated; the applicant had served her application late without having been adequately advised of the deadline at the time and their special circumstances warranted an extension of time within this time-Barcelona Institute for Advanced Social Research – and the ECtHR denied their application out of turn in the course of three separate hearings; the applicant was now facing the prospect of a possible strikeout on grounds that the application had probably overrun the stated maximum in 18 months.

The European Court of Human Rights has further reinforced the notion that the right to education is not merely an abstract concept, but a fundamental necessity for individual dignity in modern society. In the case, X v. United Kingdom, a unanimous judgment by the Grand Chamber in 2020, the Court concluded that a lack of educational guidance provided by special educational needs coordinators in Wales constituted inadequate accommodation for a child with dyslexia. The court's findings underscored the need for effective support systems within the education sector to cater to students with diverse learning needs. In light of this ruling, authorities are being urged to reassess their approaches to accommodating pupils with disabilities, recognizing that each individual requires a tailored response to achieve educational success. The ECHR emphasizes the pivotal role that teachers play in delivering inclusive education and that schools must provide resources and professionals with the necessary training to support children with diverse educational needs. By focusing on the holistic development of students, educators can help bridge the gap in educational outcomes and promote a fairer, more equitable society.

"The European Court of Human Rights recently ruled against an individual who was denied reasonable accommodation for their disability, considering the employer's assertion that the premises of the place of work were not suitable for physical mobility assistance. The court defined 'reasonable accommodation' as making adjustments that would remove a significant obstacle, but stopped short of addressing the root cause of disability, focusing instead on the existing structural barriers. In this case, they determined that financial constraints could not justify an unreasonable expectation of modification and further noted, 'the reasonable balance must be struck between the right to non-discrimination and the principle of subsidiary responsibility of the state in ensuring that disabled persons can exercise the right to a private life.' The ruling reinforced the case-law from the Court on the application of Directive 2000/78 / EC, clarifying employer liability for providing fair treatment under special circumstances deemed beyond their control. Furthermore, this judgment has provided legal context and guidance in cases where individuals struggle with reasonable accommodations following the development of complex disabilities."

Regarding the case of Dorian Webb v United Kingdom, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that the applicant's criminal conviction for manslaughter of a taxi driver should not have arisen as it was found that the killing was excusable in the circumstances. The court held that even though physical injury had to be taken into account in determining the level of provocation, it was not taken into consideration the circumstances surrounding the events in question, leading to the court determining that there was a reasonable response and reducing the applicant s criminal sentence to manslaughter.

The establishment of the right to an effective remedy by the Court of Fundamental Rights under Article 47, as it has developed through its judgments, and not only in the context of its criminal jurisdiction, can be seen as being closely linked to the effective exercise of other fundamental rights such as the freedom of thought, conscience and religion under Article 9, freedom of expression under Article 10, and right to private life under Article 8. This is particularly evident as the Court has consistently interpreted these Articles in favour of a wide and effective action, coupled to no very restrictive considerations and based, moreover, upon an assessment as to what the applicant's position would have been in a satisfactory factual situation.

The European Court of Human Rights has recently been faced with a landmark case involving the deportation of an asylum seeker named Yacoub, whose complaint alleged that he was subjected to inhumane conditions in the detention facility of the reception center in Sofia, Bulgaria. Mr. Yacoub, a 35-year-old Malian national, arrived in the EU through a refugee route by sea and lodged an application for international protection. Despite previous applications, he remained in detention due to lack of documentation and was eventually held at the Skopje Reception Centre in North Macedonia before being transferred to the reception centre in Sofia. In a historic ruling, the Court held that the detention conditions violated Article 3 of the ECHR, citing insufficient lighting, unsanitary conditions, and lack of access to basic necessities in the reception facility. As a result, Yacoub was found eligible for asylum and resided in temporary accommodation until his settlement with an EU member state was established.

In its seminal 2004 decision in Manning, the English Court of Appeal considered whether Article 2 (right to life) was breached in relation to the unlawful killing of an individual outside of the State's effective jurisdiction, holding the State responsible for breaches under section 11 of the Human Rights Act 1998 where the deprivation of life of the individual had not occurred in the State." It was to be the first decision from the ECHR to interpret the article in light of the 1999 Council of Europe Framework Decision on Addressing Violations of Human Rights in Council Framework Decision on Fighting the Proliferation of Counterfeit Medicines

The court's emphasis on ensuring that the complainants' rights under Article 14 of the European Convention are respected over that of the state in the landmark case of X v. Netherlands , ECHR (1998) 48 is still relevant today , particularly in situations where discrimination is not limited to racial or ethnic origins but also affects individuals based upon their immigration status . However similar decisions throughout history raise concerns about the scope of such measures by implying certain rights are protected .

The European Court of Human Rights has established a robust framework to balance individual rights and states' sovereignty in the context of mass surveillance. The Court has emphasised the need for states to justify any measures falling under the Article 8 right to respect for private and family life when such measures adopt strictly proportionate measures as outlined in Article 8(2). The European Court has traditionally applied flexible scrutiny to public security concerns by evaluating whether there is substantial reasons grounded in democratic or public order concerns , so states need to demonstrate that they strike an equilibrium between the competing rights outlined in Article 8(2). In numerous instances , the European Court has ruled in favour of the litigant and struck down measures deemed insufficient or entirely inappropriate with regard to proportionality to prevent overreach , demonstrating that strict proportionality would render certain security measures invalid due to arbitrary powers not justified under any justification for the interference under Articles 10 or 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The European Court of Human Rights, situated in the heart of Strasbourg, held a hearing yesterday in the case of X and Y against the State of France; with judges deliberating on the extent to which a national law permits law enforcement officers to undertake surveillance measures in public places, not dissimilar to video cameras in local streets, which can potentially infringe on the fundamental right to conduct private conversations.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to a fair trial as a fundamental principle of justice, regardless of the circumstances in which the case is being heard. One such landmark case was that of "Kjeldran Abdellaoui (2012) 56" , where the Court reaffirmed the importance of protecting the rights of accused persons by requiring them to be informed of their right to legal assistance throughout the trial process. This particular ruling underscores the significance of continued vigilance by the ECHR in safeguarding the principles of justice and upholding the rights of those accused in the legal system . In addition , the ECHR has also emphasized the need for the prosecution to present its case in a clear and concise manner , as highlighted in the case of " Dritac v Croatia (2013) 61" ( Court of Justice Series 4 ) , which exemplifies the ECHR 's commitment to ensuring that all parties involved in a trial are given a fair opportunity to present their arguments in their own vigorous yet coherent and well-supported manner .

The European Court of Human Rights established the right to effective access to courts and fair procedures for resolving disputes under Article 6 of the European Convention, in contrast to the European Convention on Human Rights itself. This guarantee confers upon each person affected by such proceedings the right to vindicate against injustice or hardship for unjust decisions, thereby protecting them from public authorities who are seen. Through such proceedings within the court — often supported by judges — it serves the effective protection of the rights enshrined in the founding document and ensures the functioning of a just and fair legal system.

The ECHR has established a series of seminal precedents that frame the judicial assessment of freedom of expression, most notably in Worriskowski v Poland and @s of Schümer v Italy, in which the Court reiterated that abstract speech cannot be proscribed, provided it does not relate to any events or facts of 'national importance'; rather than to maintain a framework for authorisation under the Article 10 limitation.

In the landmark case of Chamberlain v United Kingdom, the ECHR ruled that no exceptional circumstances justified interference with a suspended prisoner's right to humane treatment under Article 3, as it was not sufficiently established that conditions in the prison fostered torture or inhuman or degrading treatment, contrary to Article 3; the court merely imposed a partial suspension not amounting to total deprivations of liberty necessary to prevent an imminent and substantial risk of ill-treatment .

"In light of the ECHR's judgment in Smith and Others v United Kingdom, it is clear that the Strasbourg court recognised the intricate complexities of social inequality in 2018. The case illustrated how institutional barriers can hinder access to justice for disadvantaged groups and underscored the need for measures to prevent further discrimination. Moreover, the court's decision reinforced its commitment to upholding the principles of equality and non-discrimination as outlined in Article 14 of the European Convention. The case therefore serves as a catalyst for reflection on how effective remedies can be implemented in practice. Furthermore, the judgment highlights the urgent need for greater awareness and education on issues surrounding discrimination, enabling all individuals, particularly marginalised groups, to navigate the court system with confidence. The findings of this case will resonate with contemporary discussions on equality and justice, serving as a powerful reminder of the ongoing struggle towards a more equitable society."

In contrast, her application, while not solely based on the present facts, laid bare the systematic and deeply entrenched discrimination afflicting her, thus preventing her from leading a normal life in a country where her fundamental human rights are continually being eroded.

In a groundbreaking move, the ECHR has ruled in favor of a group of elderly French citizens whose lifelong pension contributions were incorrectly discounted due to the bureaucratic mishandling of their records. Ruling in a 4–2 verdict citing European Union directives emphasizing equal treatment under the law and protecting the rights of all pensioners, the court declared the government's actions to have violated the principle of non-discrimination as stipulated in Article 21 of the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms. The ruling could have significant implications for other European countries battling similar cases of pension age discrimination, and experts have welcomed the decision as a major victory for those fighting for fairness and equal access to social services.

The Court of Arbitration for Sports (CAS) is a three-member panel that oversees cases involving international sports. The court is made up of a president and two other judges who are appointed by the International Olympic Committee. The court has jurisdiction over cases involving the rules and sanctions of the International Olympic Committee, the International Paralympic Committee and the International Federation of the sport in question. The court's decisions are based solely on the application of the rules of the International Federation. Decisions rendered by the CAS may be appealed to the European Court of Human Rights, if the parties are from member states of the Council of Europe. In 1921 the court had jurisdiction over most international sports competitions where competitors from multiple nations were present; from 2004 the court's jurisdiction over events not governed by an International Federation was limited to cases where individuals have first contested it in domestic courts. In cases decided under sports regulation 2014/8 of the CAS, individuals from outside the European Economic Area that wish to contest their decisions are free to appeal to the European Sports Court of Arbitration, unless otherwise agreed by both parties.

The concept of jurisdiction in English law has long been a subject of contention, particularly in the context of the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR). The ECHR is structured around the concept of state liability, where the state may be held accountable for its actions or failure to act in relation to the violation of individual rights and freedoms. In recent years, the ECHR's jurisdiction has been subject to a number of challenges, with an increasing number of cases brought by states asserting their sovereign immunity from liability for acts or omissions committed prior to the entry into force of the ECHR itself. This argument, however, has been largely rejected by the Court, which has consistently held that such claims are incompatible with the very fabric of human rights. Furthermore, the notion of jurisdiction in the ECHR encompasses not only the actual exercise of state power but also the omissions and failures in doing so. The Court has consistently emphasized that the state has a duty to act , including those acts or omissions that might be considered simply "neither here nor there". This extends the concept of jurisdiction far beyond the realm of overt actions, instead covering an array of issues including duty of care, state liability and failure. "The use of Article 6 Section 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights by a UK court to review decisions by a domestic court remains a contentious issue, with judgments frequently highlighting the need for greater consistency in the application of this fundamental right. The European Court of Human Rights has consistently recognised the right to a fair trial as an instrumental aspect of the overall right to a fair process, and in Bonnelli v Italy (2003) a broader reading of Article 6 was accepted, whereby every stage of the proceedings shall be fair and open to the prosecution and defence. A fair trial extends to encompass, within the process itself, those aspects which are essential for its integrity, such as the right to legal representation counsel and the possibility of defence. In addition to the conditions specified in Article 10 of the Convention, adequate facilities shall be ensured for the conduct of proceedings in a manner consistent with the fair determination of reality, ensuring the integrity of procedure by excluding possible prejudices from any party involved.

The court clarified the framework for Article 141 of the European Convention on Human Rights by stating that failure to provide reasonable accommodation can amount to discrimination under the equality clause. This precept is especially pertinent to individuals who suffer from Disabilities, as the ruling seeks to uphold their right to equal treatment and access to employment opportunities. By declaring that a lack of reasonable adjustment amounts to discrimination, the ECHR underscores the need for effective strategies to break down obstacles in the job market and promote inclusivity. The case serves as a potent reminder that the ECHR continues to play a pivotal role in safeguarding human rights, particularly those of individuals who face disproportionately high barriers to employment.
