In finding in favour of the applicant, the European Court of Human Rights will consider whether the decisions taken by the applicant, in whole or in part, in the exercise of their rights under 'the Convention as contemplated by Article 9 of that Convention amount to discrimination on grounds of religious affiliation , prohibited by Article 9 of the Convention .

Upon reviewing the Court of Human Rights' advisory proceedings, I found that the question of whether the introduction of a 60-megabyte database on crimes against humanity perpetuates the system of impunity relied on by international criminal tribunals has been subject to considerable legal debate. The ECHR recently heard an appeal by Austria concerning an investigative mission linked to the database for monitoring torture, with Austria contending that the creation of the database under the principles of law enforcement cooperation may impede or hinder an effective control mechanism, such as human oversight.

Criticising the United Kingdom's pre-charge detention provisions in Article 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights, a human rights watchdog has accused the British government of fuelling mistrust towards the judiciary. The European Court of Human Rights ruled that the pre-charge detention policy had breached Article 5(5) of the European Convention on Human Rights, which provides that individuals may not be detained in strict security concerns, as long as they are brought before the competent authorities within 24 hours.

On 21 January 2003, Mrs. X, a resident of Nicosia, Cyprus, and a member of the Greek Cypriot community, brought a complaint to the ECtHR, alleging that the impugned detention had, inter alia, breached her right to freedom of movement under Article 2 of the ECHR. The case, that followed, revolved around X's detention upon her entry to the territory of Cyprus, where X had travelled from Turkey and took up residence without her valid passport, having been granted administrative residence in Cyprus in 2000, and later applying for Cypriot citizenship in 2002 on the basis of her ethnic Cypriot background.

The Commission of Human Rights in Bosnia and Herzegovina requested that the European Court of Human Rights review their request to remove a 2015 case regarding the rights of individuals expelled from the Srebrenica massacre, citing collective criminal responsibility of war crimes under ECHR Article 7 and collective persecution under Article 8, alleging that by removing the case the Commission violated the case law established in the cases of Smiljánĭcić and Šarić v. Bosnia and Herzegovina, ECtHR Chamber, 21 July 2005 and Spasojević v. Bosnia and Herzegovina, ECtHR Chamber, 27 October 2008 respectively.

In the seminal case of <mark>Khodorkovsky v. Russia</mark>, the European Court of Human Rights underscored the importance of the principle of non-enumerability in Article 5 – neither the ECHR nor its individual member states can dictate when and where someone is entitled to protection against arbitrary detention, even in extraordinary circumstances.

The ECHR's judgments provide a unique window into the complex workings of the Convention's functioning, enabling scholars and practitioners to engage more deeply with the evolution of human rights law at the international level.

In a landmark decision issued on 3 January 2019, the ECHR found in favour of Ms. Janko against the Republic of Poland in Case of Janko against Poland, relating to the right to effective remedy for the unlawful detention under Article 4 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The Court re-affirmed its previous judgment in the case of Kurtagin v. Ukraine, by concluding that a failed asylum claimant's subsequent detention without effective judicial authorisation for expulsion must have served as an unlawful detention, under the circumstances of the case.

The European Court of Human Rights has long been a crucial institution in safeguarding the fundamental rights and liberties of individuals across Europe, but recent developments in the role of the court have sparked debate among legal scholars, calling for a reevaluation of its impact on the progressive erosion of national sovereignty. Critics argue that the court's increasing use of direct effect doctrine undermines the ability of member states to make decisions in the best interest of their citizens, whereas proponents counter that the doctrine serves as a necessary safeguard against discriminatory practices and ensures that judges are bound by treaty commitments, thereby upholding the rule of law. As the ECHR's powers continue to expand, it remains to be seen how the delicate balance between national and supranational authorities will respond in adapting to the evolving landscape, with the ultimate direction of the institution hanging precariously in the balance.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled in favour of a whistleblower who exposed the government's alleged involvement in a scandal involving the collection of personal data without consent. The judgment, delivered by a panel of judges on a complaint from the complainant, a Belgian journalist, states that the collection of data by the government agency was in breach of the protection afforded by Article 8 of the Convention, which guarantees the right to privacy. The court also found that the agency had failed to take adequate measures to prevent the misuse of the collected data, which had been used for employment checks and recruitment purposes. The verdict is seen as a significant ruling, as it establishes a clearer basis for protecting whistleblowers who could face retaliation for exposing government wrongdoing.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to administrative procedure as articulated in Article 41 of the European Convention, upholding the significance of a fair and impartial hearing in ensuring public trust in the administration of justice.

The European Court of Human Rights recently ruled in the case of X v United Kingdom, finding that the British government had exceeded its duty to protect the fundamental human right of freedom of assembly. The court argued that the police actions at the mass protest in London had placed an unlawful restriction on the ability of demonstrators to exercise their right to peaceful assembly. The ECHR declared that the police had deployed an excessive use of force against unarmed protesters, resulting in the arrest of several individuals. In its ruling, the court emphasized the importance of balancing national security and individual rights, stating that the protection of freedom of assembly is a cornerstone of democratic societies. The government would have to review their counter-protest policies to ensure that they do not unjustly trample the fundamental rights of those exercising their right to peaceful assembly.

In a landmark decision, the ECHR reinforced the distinction between victim compensation and legal damages, stating that a victim's compensation should not amount to a "perfunctory payment" and must be "fair and civilised in substance" by analogy with legal damages for personal injury not as a criminal deterrent.

On 4 March 2015, the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights delivered judgment in the case @@ @ @ Rasaud v @@ @ United Kingdom . In a nutshell , the Court ruled that the decision of the first instance tribunal not to admit evidence about the effect of hearing the case meant that the applicant had suffered a positive violation of Article 6 ( 1 ) which guarantees an individual right to a fair trial , without time for examination of evidence , and although the government did not actually intend to exclude a fair examination with full opportunity , Article 6 was violated nonetheless . This case therefore vindicated the rights and interests in Article 6 .

The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) was established in 1949, succeeding the former European Commission of Human Rights. Commissioned by the Council of Europe, the ECHR is responsible for enforcing the European Convention of Human Rights, a set of fundamental human rights that seek to protect against oppression, suffering, and injustice. The court operates at Strasbourg, France, and it is the final stage in a multi-nature judicial process where an individual or group can challenge a national court decision. They can appeal to an ECHR judgement of 'adverse outcome' or decision if human rights were incorrectly decided against them.

"Though the European Court of Human Rights has recognised that any generalised and unfounded accusations in the media can undermine an individual's privacy, it also clarified that the European Convention requires an establishment of reasonable grounds for such claims before they can be examined in the courts."

In a recent significant ruling, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) asserted its jurisdiction over a case involving a Turkish individual who claimed that the mass expulsion of migrants from Syria by Greece in 2022 violated the right to non-discrimination enshrined in Article 14 of the European Convention. The court stated that the Greek government's actions were precluded by EU law, as they targeted an entire national group rather than individuals for deportation. The court thus upheld the case, ruling that the applicant had a well-founded complaint that was not manifestly unjust and not incompatible with the ECHR.

The European Court of Human Rights ruling that the law is not an end in itself but a means to ensure human rights protection raises questions regarding the adequacy of the European Convention on Human Rights in light of emerging technological advancements. In its recent judgement, the court clarified that the prohibition of discrimination must be interpreted in the light of the current state of technology, recognizing the evolving nature of human rights and social norms.

The judgment of the European Court of Human Rights in the case of Kokkinis and others v Greece delivered on 17 December 1996, related to the treatment of persons in pre-trial detention by Greek authorities. In this case, the court held that persons awaiting trial must be afforded the right to protection of their personal and family life whilst in detention, and that the measures governing the duration of detention should be framed in accordance with relevant law and the decision of the Court and should be strictly applied. This ruling clarifies the notion of the minimum rights to be guaranteed to those of whom they are detained prior to the final judgment under the European Convention standards and provides an application for general clarity on the nature of the measures relating to detention of persons in custody for questioning pertaining to criminal or administrative cases - it underlines the distinction between detention which is necessary but deemed proportionate and that which is not so deemed.

In a landmark decision, the European Court of Human Rights recently ruled that the rights enumerated in Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights to freedom of expression, although not absolute, entail an obligation to ensure it is available to all individuals in the public realm, including in mass media to enable the formation of public opinion. The court rejected a complaint brought by a Bulgarian national who had been barred from accessing online information concerning a recent parliamentary vote by her government in response to her public criticism, deeming the restriction to be disproportionate to the need to maintain constitutional secrecy. In doing so, the court acknowledged the need for balanced information to be able to be available to promote public understanding and hold elected representatives accountable, while recognizing that a ban on certain information, without clear justification, could lead to undermining of democratic processes.

"The European Court of Human Rights has long upheld the unwavering commitment to protecting the rights of individuals who suffer at the hands of their own governments. Through landmark decisions such as Smith and Wales v United Kingdom and Norris v Ireland, the court has demonstrated its unwavering resolve to safeguard the fundamental rights of all human beings in the face of state control, whether that be through suppression of free speech, prohibition of political assembly, or deprivation of life and liberty. The binding precedent established by the 'right to life' in Douglas and Reid, and its subsequent extension to include the 'right to physical and psychological integrity', has signified the ECHR's utmost priority in safeguarding the vulnerable and ensuring that states bear the burden of responsibility for their actions. Those who challenge these rights in the court do so at their own peril, for it is the duty of bodies like the European Court of Human Rights to scrutinise the actions of states and uphold the high standards of human dignity, enshrined in the Convention's sweeping jurisprudence."

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently demonstrated that the general legal principle of non-discrimination under Article 14 of the European Convention can be effectively implemented through a positive action adopted by a contracting state, such as authorising its administrative authorities or public service providers to train staff to treat an individual differently if they possess effective reasons arising from particular personal circumstances, where this differentiation does not trigger an inequality that could be resolved by general arrangements under existing laws, or where such equality of treatment therefore presents a proportionate means to secure equal treatment of all individuals regardless of their personal background.

"In December 2017, the Grand Chamber of the ECHR unanimously held that the detention of Polish nationals under the 2008 state-of-emergency restrictions amounts to a violation of Article 5 (Protection against prejudice of persons remaining in the nationality of the place where they are recognised as refugees) of the European Convention of Human Rights . The court based its decision primarily on the requirement of effective domestic remedies (para 30 ) and the prohibition of arbitrary detention (para 73 ) , and to some extent also Articles 10 ( freedom of expression ) and 18 ( Non-discrimination ) . While the majority of the Grand Chamber in Sturgeon v . the UK (ECHR 2013 , para 112 ) held that the requirements of European Convention Article 5 are not liable to be complied with without effective public inquiry into the circumstances surrounding the detention , in the present case the court considered that the public hearing and inquiry into the arrest and detention of the applicants had fallen short due to the lack of a comprehensive and effective investigation particularly as regards the content of the explanations provided by the police , of the identification of the individual involved in the alleged acts , and generally due to the unauthorised activities of the investigating officers , due to absence of the effective witness consent , and non
